title: Inheritance
description: Master Java inheritance — extends, super(), method hiding, and the is-a relationship — the way CSE110 exams test it, with full tracing exercises.

sections.content: Inheritance questions appear in **every** CSE110 exam format — output prediction, error identification, and short-answer tracing. The exam tests whether you know the difference between `super()` and `this()`, how constructor chains run, when method hiding applies vs overriding, and what `instanceof` returns for multi-level hierarchies. This chapter builds every rule you need with traceable code examples.

Inheritance models the **is-a relationship**: a `Dog` *is-a* `Animal`, a `SavingsAccount` *is-a* `BankAccount`. In Java, `class Dog extends Animal` means Dog **inherits** all non-private fields and methods of Animal, and can **add** new ones or **override** inherited behavior.

sections.label: Java's Single Inheritance Rule
sections.content: Java allows **single inheritance only** — a class can `extends` exactly one parent class. However, a class can implement multiple interfaces (covered in Ch13). If no `extends` clause is written, the class implicitly extends `Object`.

sections.title: Defining a Parent and Child Class

sections.content: When `new Dog("Rex", 3, "Lab")` is called, the output order is: `Animal constructor: Rex` then `Dog constructor: Lab`. The **parent constructor always runs first** because `super(...)` must be the first statement in the child constructor.

rows: Parent must have a no-arg constructor
Must explicitly call matching parent constructor
Calls sibling constructor; super chain triggered from there
`this()` and `super()` cannot both be on line 1

sections.label: 🚨 Exam Trap: Missing super() When Parent Has No Default Constructor
sections.content: If the parent class defines **only** a parameterized constructor (no no-arg constructor), and the child does not explicitly call `super(args)`, the code will **not compile**. The error is: *'implicit super constructor Animal() is undefined — must explicitly invoke another constructor'*. This is one of the most-tested compile-time errors in CSE110.

**Overriding** applies to **instance methods**: the child class provides a new implementation. At runtime, the JVM uses the **actual object type** to decide which version runs (dynamic dispatch). **Hiding** applies to **static methods**: if a child defines a static method with the same signature, it *hides* (not overrides) the parent's version. Which version runs depends on the **declared type**, not the actual type.

sections.title: Override vs Hide — Spot the Difference

sections.content: `super` has two uses: (1) **`super(args)`** in a constructor to call the parent constructor; (2) **`super.method()`** or **`super.field`** in a method body to access the parent's version that was overridden.

sections.title: Using super() and super.method() Together

sections.content: Java supports multi-level inheritance (A → B → C). When `new C()` is called, the constructor chain runs **top-down**: A's constructor first, then B's, then C's. This is guaranteed by the `super()` call requirement.

sections.title: Three-Level Constructor Chain Trace

sections.label: Field Shadowing in Inherited Classes
sections.content: If a child class declares a field with the **same name** as a parent field, the child's field *shadows* (not overrides) the parent's. Both fields exist; which one is accessed depends on the **declared type** of the reference. This is a subtle but commonly tested trap — avoid naming conflicts in real code.

The `instanceof` operator tests whether an object is an instance of a class **or any of its ancestors**. For a reference `Dog d = new Dog()`: `d instanceof Dog` → true, `d instanceof Animal` → true, `d instanceof Object` → true. It always returns false for null.

sections.title: instanceof in an Inheritance Hierarchy

items: **Constructors** — they are not inherited; each class must define its own
**`private` members** — not directly accessible in the child (though they exist in memory)
**`static` members** are inherited in the sense that they're accessible, but they can only be hidden, not overridden
**`final` methods** cannot be overridden in any subclass

sections.title: Exercise 1: Constructor Chain Order
sections.explanation: Trace: 1) `new Top()` calls `Top()`. 2) `Top()` has no explicit super/this, so compiler inserts `super()` → calls `Mid()`. 3) `Mid()` has `this(10)` on line 1 → calls `Mid(10)`. 4) `Mid(10)` calls `super(10)` → `Base(10)`. 5) `Base(10)` prints 'Base(10)', returns. 6) Back in `Mid(10)`: prints 'Mid(10)', returns. 7) Back in `Mid()`: `this(10)` chain done, continues → prints 'Mid()', returns. 8) Back in `Top()`: prints 'Top'. Final output: Base(10) / Mid(10) / Mid() / Top.

sections.title: Exercise 2: Override vs Hide with Polymorphic Reference
sections.explanation: Line 1 `obj.show()`: declared type is A, actual type is B. `show()` is an instance method that is **overridden** in B → dynamic dispatch → 'B.show()'. Line 2 `obj.print()`: `print()` is **static** → method hiding, not overriding → resolved by DECLARED type (A) → 'A.print()'. Line 3 `obj2.show()`: actual type is B, overridden → 'B.show()'. Line 4 `obj2.print()`: declared type is B, static → resolved by declared type B → 'B.print()'.

sections.title: Exercise 3: Missing super() With No Default Constructor
sections.error: Line 18 (Manager constructor): Compile-time error — implicit super constructor Employee() is undefined. The Employee class has only a parameterized constructor Employee(String, double), so the compiler cannot insert a no-arg super() call automatically. Manager() must explicitly call super(name, salary).
sections.explanation: When a parent class defines only parameterized constructors, Java does NOT auto-generate a no-arg constructor. Since Manager() provides no explicit super(...) call, the compiler tries to insert super() and fails because Employee() does not exist. Fix: add the missing parameters to Manager's constructor and explicitly call super(name, salary) as the first statement. This is a **compile-time** error.

sections.title: Exercise 4: Calling super() After this()
sections.error: Line 12: Compile-time error — call to super() must be the first statement in a constructor. super("Car") is placed after this.doors=doors on line 11, which is illegal in Java.
sections.explanation: Java strictly requires that `super(...)` or `this(...)` is the **very first statement** in a constructor body, before any other code including field assignments. Moving `this.doors = doors` after `super("Car")` resolves the compile error. This is a **compile-time** error.

sections.title: Exercise 5: Multi-Level Override Chain Trace
sections.answer:
Hello from X
Hello from Y
Hello from Y and Z
sections.explanation: The `run()` method is only defined in X and calls `greet()`. Because `greet()` is a virtual (instance) method, it uses dynamic dispatch based on the **actual** runtime type. (1) `a.run()`: actual type X → `greet()` returns 'Hello from X'. (2) `b.run()`: `run()` inherited from X; inside `run()`, `this` is a Y object → `greet()` dispatches to Y → 'Hello from Y'. (3) `c.run()`: `run()` inherited from X; `this` is Z → `greet()` in Z calls `super.greet()` which is Y's `greet()` = 'Hello from Y', concatenated with ' and Z' → 'Hello from Y and Z'.

sections.title: Exercise 6: Build a 3-Level Inheritance Hierarchy
sections.approach: Each constructor must call super() with the right args. Dog.makeSound() overrides Animal.makeSound(). We need a way to call the original Animal version — use super.super? No: Java doesn't allow super.super. We can call Pet's version, but Pet doesn't override it, so super.makeSound() from Dog calls up to Animal. Create an Animal ref pointing to the Dog to demonstrate the original implementation is accessible.
sections.explanation: The constructor chain for Dog: Dog() → super() = Pet(name,sound,owner) → super() = Animal(name,sound). Each level sets its own field. Dog.makeSound() is an override — dynamic dispatch always calls it when the actual type is Dog, even through an Animal reference. To call Animal.makeSound() from outside the class without an Animal object, you'd need to be inside Dog's code using `super.makeSound()`. The `@Override` annotation ensures a compile error if the signature doesn't match the parent, protecting against typos.

items: **`extends`**: declares inheritance; Java allows only one parent class
**`super(args)`**: calls parent constructor; must be the **first statement** in child constructor
**`super.method()`**: calls the parent's overridden version of a method from inside a child method
**Constructor chain order**: top-most ancestor first, child last (A → B → C)
**Overriding**: instance methods; resolved at **runtime** by actual object type (dynamic dispatch)
**Hiding**: static methods; resolved at **compile time** by declared reference type
**`@Override`**: annotation to verify at compile time that overriding is correct; use always
**`instanceof`**: returns true if object is an instance of that class OR any ancestor class
**NOT inherited**: constructors, `private` fields/methods, `final` method bodies (can't override)
**Field shadowing**: child field with same name shadows parent field; resolved by declared type
